Re:
Fern PolrrrcAt- PRACrrcEs CoMMrssroN
.128 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA gigl4_232g
(916) 322-5660 o Fax (916) 322-0886
September 15, 2008
Colleen Finnegan
Community Arts Coordinator
City of Carlsbad
1200 Carlsbad Village Drive
Carlsbad. California 92008- I 989
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. ,{-08-157
I The Political Refirrm Act is contained in Government Code Sections til0(x) throu-eh 9l()1.1. ,,\ll
stlltuk)ry rc'f'erence's are kr the Government Code. unless otherrvise indicated. Thc' r.egulati.ni .f thc, Fair
Political Practices Commission are conttined in Sections llil l0 through ltJ997 of Title I of the Calitirrnia
Code of Regulations. ,\ll rc'uulatory ret'erences are to Title l. Division-6 of the California Codc.f
Regulations. unless otherwise inclicated.
Dear Ms. Finnegan:
This letter responds to yolrr request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").I
QUESTION
As a designated employee of the City of Carlsbad, may you accept a gift of travel,
lodging and subsistence from a government agency of the clty of Futtsu, Japin for a trip
where you will be representing the City of caitsuad at a twenty-year anniversary
celebration of the sister city relationship between the cities of Cirlsbad and Futtsu? If so,
must the gifts be reported?
CONCLUSION
The gift of travel and relatecl loclging and subsistence from the Futtsu government
is not subject to the gift limit because it is reasonably related to a legislative oi
go!'ernmental purpose' However, the gifts are disclosable on yoLlr almual statement of
economic interests and subject to the conflict-of-interest provi.sions of the Act unless
another exception applies, as clescribecl helow.
q:?


File No. A-08-157
Page No. 2
You are the Community Arts Coordinator for the City of Carlsbad, a position
designated in the city's conflict-of-interest code. For the past 18 years you have been the
city staff liaison to the city's Sister City Committee. In that role, you have been invited
by the Futtsu International Relations Association ("FIRA"), of Fuitsu, Japan, to attend
and speak at the 20th Anniversary Sister City Celebrations celebrating the sister city
relationship between the two cities. In your letter you describe FIR,{as o non-p.oiit
organization of citizens of Futtsu whose purpose is to manage the sister cities
relationships between Futtsu and its international partners. Ho*"u.r, you subsequently
clarified the status ofIIRA by sending Lrs a copy of u lett". from the Ciairperson of
FIRA indicating that FIRA is a government organization. Your trip will include a
ceremony to be held on one day at which you will speak and an official tour of
community facilities, including schools and local government buildings, on the next day.
FIRA has offered to pay for your round-trip transportation between Los Angeles and
Futtsu, three days and three nights of lodging in Futtsu and food and bever{es during the
your visit' If you decide to travel to other parts of Japan after the official tri[ is over, you
will do so at your own expense.
FACTS
ANALYSIS
Gifts Generally
- 
The Act imposes certain obligations on public officials regarding the receipt of
gifts. First, Section 89503(c) provides a gift limit for designated emplolees of local
govemment agencies. Second, the Act requires that every local govern.n"nt og"r.y
adopt a conflict-of-interest code designating categories oi employees who must disclose
their economic interests, including specified donors of gifts aggregating to $50 or more
from the same source, that could foreseeably be financiitty arrectJa by-the exercise of
their duties. (Section 87300.) Third, Section 87100 requirls that public officials
disqualify themselves from any governmental decision that will have a foreseeable and
material financial effect.on a donor of gifts aggregating $390 or more in value frovided
to, received by, or promised to the public official within l2 months prior to the time when
the decision is made. (Section 87103(e).) Generally, when an official receives a payment
or reimbursement for travel, including transportation and related meals and lodging, that
payment or reimbursement is considered to be a gift2 unless an exception appliJs. Below,
we discuss the gift rules applicable to your travel.
r Section tt2028(a) essentially provides that a gift includes any payment. including
reimbursement. that confers a personal benefit to the exient equal rlr gr"r,.. consideration ii not provided in
return' An ofTicial who claims that a payment is not a gifi by i.ururif providing equal or greater
consideration has the burden ofproving so.


File No. A-08-157
Page No.3
Gift Limit - Exception for Travel Payments
Section 89503(c) provides, in pertinent part, that no designated employee of a
local elected government agency may accept gifts from any single sollrce in any calendar
year worth in excess of the gift limit if the official would be required to report the receipt
of income or gifts from that source on his or her statement of economic inierests. The
clrrrent gift limit is $390. (Regulation 18940.2.) As a designated employee of the City of
Carlsbad, you are required to disclose the name and address of each source of a gift oi
$50 or more received during the previons l2 months. (Sections 87300, Regulation
18730.)
Payments for travel, however, are not subject to the gift limit if permitted by
section 89506, which provides, in pertinent part, that payments, advancis, or
reimbursements for travel, including actual transportation and related lodging and
subsistence, that is reasonably related to a legislative or governmentat purpoie are not
prohibited or limited if the travel is provided by a govemment, a govemmental agency, a
foreign government, or a governmental authority. (Section 89506(aX2).) If a gift of
travel meets these requirements, it is not subject to the annual gift limit of $390 per
source. (Regulation 1 8942(bXt).)
The issue is whether your trip to Futtsu, Japan is reasonably related to a
legislative or governmental purpose. The city of Futtsu is a sister city of Carlsbad and
yoll are a designated representative for this relationship between Carlsbad and Futtsu.
While in Futtsut, you will attend a celebration ceremony at which you will deliver a
speech and will be taken on an official tour of government facilities.
We have advised that travel expenses provided to a county supervisor to explore a
sister city relationship with a province in China was reasonably related to a legislative or
govemmental purpose. (Bagatelos Advice Letter, No. ,{-93-309.) Similarly, we advised
that a travel payment made for the pLlrpose of sending a representative of the Governor's
office to a signing ceremony of a sister state was also reasonably related to a legislative
or govemmental purpose. (Wong Advice Letter, No. 4-96-297.) We have also advised
that the reimbursement of travel expenses provided to an employee of a mayor's office to
further the goal of a sister city agreement was also reasonably related to a legislative or
governmental purpose. (Sylvict Advice Letter, No. A- 97-139.) Your trip to Futtsu is
similar to those in the cited advice letters in that you have been invited fo represent your
city to further the goals of the sister city relationship of the two cities. We conclude that
your trip to Futtsu, Japan is reasonably related to a legislative or governmental purpose.
Thus, the payments for transportation to and from Futtsu and relaied lodging and
sttbsistence are gifts that are not subject to the $390 gift limit. How.u.., ru.h gifts are
reportable on your statement of economic interests (Form 700) and are subjectio the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act unless onother ext eptiort ctpplie.s.lSection
87207(c), Regulation 18950. I (b).)


File No. A-08-157
. 
Page No.4
Exception for Travel in Connection with Speeches
Regulation 18950.3 provides a limited exemption from both the gift limits and
gift reporting requirements for "free admission, and refreshments and siirilar non-cash
benefits provided to a filer during the entire event at which the filer gives a speech,
participates in a panel or seminar, or provides a similar service...onJory n.."rro.y
lodging and subsistence provided directly in connection with the speechlpanel, seminar,
or service, including but not limited to meals and beverages on the day of the
activity
We have previously advised that Regulation 18950.3 generally exempts necessary
meals and beverages on the day of the speech and necessury l,odgirg on the d'ay of the
speech, and possibly the day before the speech and the day after-the-speech if necessary
due to travel time- (Gaulr Advice Letter, No. A-07-158, citing to AllenAdvice Letter, No.
I-94-270 and Nishite Advice Letter No. A-91-568.) Any accommodations outside these
parameters may be considered gifts subject to normal reporting mles and gift limits,
unless another exception applies.
Exception for Informational Material
lnformational material is expressly excluded from the definition of gift. Section
82028 provides that the term "gift" does not include "informational material such as
books, reports, pamphlets, calendars, or periodicals." It also provides that ..ro puyr"r,
for travel or reimbursement for any expenses shall be deemed 'informational material.,,,
(Section 82028(bX1).)
The Commission has interpreted "informational material" to include informational
tours' (ln re Spellman (1975) I FPPC Ops. 6.)a An inforrnational tour falls within the gift
exception if it is specifically designed for the purpose of assisting the public official in
the performance of his or her official duties. (Regulation l8g42.f (").1' Informational
material may include on-site demonstrations, tou-rs, or inspections designed specifically
for public officials. (Briggs Advice Letter, No. 4-93-2t0.) we have looked at several
factors when determining whether a tour is indeed informational material, including the
fact that the tour will_primarily convey information, the tour is specifically designed for
public officials, and the tour was formally structured to convey information to,ir"
officials. we also look to see that the tour does not appear extravagant in value or nature
in relationship to the information conveyed. (BctrkerAdvice Letter, No. l-93-1g6.)
3 The exception contained in Regulation 18950.3 does not apply tn a gifi of transportation outside
the United States even in connection with delivering a speech. panel oi similar service.
I Theacquisitionofadequateinfbrmationisessential tosoundlegislativeandadministrative
action. It is not the purpose of the Political Retbrm Act to interf'ere in any way in the fiee flow of
intirrmation to officials- only financial pressures and inducements are soughl to be Iimited and disclosed.
(ln re Spel lman. supra.)


File No. A-08-157
Page No.5
Applying these factors to the tour of government facilities, it appears that your totr of
government facilities is specifically designed for this visit of pubti. officials and is
strllctured to provide information of interest to public officiali parlicipating in the sister
city program. As your city's representative for this program, we believe that the tour will
indeed be informational material.
However' travel or transportation provided to a public official in connection with
a tollr does not fall within this exclusion unless such transportation is not commercially
available. (Section 8202S(bXl).) Air transportation to ond f.o* Japan is widely available
commercially, as would, we presume, ground transportation to youi various destinations
in Japan. Consequently, absent additional information, any transportation provided to
you in connection with this travel will not fall within the "informational material"
exception.5
Summary
Under the exception for travel in connection with making a speech, free
admission to the ceremony where you will deliver the speech, ."fr.rh*.nts served at the
ceremony, meals and beverages consumed on that day and necessary lodging are not
gifts. (Lodging for the day before and after the speec-h are also not gifts f, rJ...rury fo,
travel time.) These are not subject to the gift limits and do not need-to be reported on
your Form 700. (Regulation 18950.3.) The tour of govemment facilities is not a gift
because it is an informational tour. We presume, abient additional facts, that all
transportation in connection with the tour is a reportable gift because it is commercially
obtainable. In that case, the transportation is reportable btit not subject to the gift limits.
(Section 89506; Regulation 189501.1(b).)
Under Section 89506(aX2), payment by FIRA for transporration to and from, and
ground transportation in, Futtsn is not subject to the gift limit but is reportable on your
Form 700. with respect ro lodging, if you are provided free lodging beyond that
permitted under Regulation 18950.3, the cost thereof would not be iubiect to the gift lirnit
but would be reportable. Similarly, the cost of meals and beverages ("subsistence,,)
beyond that permitted under I8950.3 (meals and beverages on days other than the day
5 we have previctusly advised that aerial and bus tours tilrmally structured to sonvey information
to an ofllcial, particularly where the donor will not be appearing before the agency. nor will be otherwise
impacted tinancially by the a,tency's decision, and wheie the t<iur appeared to be a reasonable means ro
convey the information, dld not appear extravagant in value or natuie in relationship to the infbrmation
conveyed. and did not involv'e tlther benetlts. dekrurs or other excursions. were infirrmational krurs
e.rcludedtiomthedeflnitionofa.'giti." \RutlolpltAdviceLerrer.No.A_01-220;VillirrcsAdviceLetter.
No. I-0-5-20 I : Barker Adv,ice Leter. No. I-9J_ I tt6. )


File No. A-08-157
Page No. 6
yon will be delivering yolrr speech) are nor subject ro the $390 gift limit but are
reportable on Form 700.
Any transportation, lodging, meals, beverages, free admissions to concerts. galas,
banquets, or other events that do not fall within the above described exceptions are
reportable gifts subjecr ro rhe gift limits.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
-7r/k o*).- 2ff7--
By: Valentina Joyce
Counsel, Legal Division
vJjgl

lodging and subsistence from a government agency of the clty of Futtsu, Japin for a trip
